Task id: T74 (ledgerRefs goals:G5) — ROUND 2 re-dispatch after review.

Branch implement/T74 (HEAD a66df7505c910d265d170f3eb522cf336ee4172f), checked out in worktree /home/pavel/work/safe/fecalia/.claude/worktrees/agent-afa803909d4c484b6. Work THERE if reachable; else `git switch -c t74-fix implement/T74` in your own worktree via the shared .git store, commit, report the EXACT sha (copy from git output).

Go repo — gate `go build ./... && go vet ./... && go test ./internal/device/... -race && go test ./...` via `nix develop -c`.

Prior-round reviewer criticism to address (1 item):
1. [fable] The R70 first-resolve install path is never exercised through the PRODUCTION wiring: no test invokes startFailoverAndResolution, and TestFirstResolveInstallsEndpointAndInitiatesHandshake hand-wires ctrl.install = deviceInstallEndpoint(...) itself, duplicating instead of driving the load-bearing line in failover.go (ctrl.install = deviceInstallEndpoint(dev, peer.PublicKey, hlg)) and the boot.resolver/boot.specs handoff through startResolution. Because installActive silently falls back to SetPeerRemote when install == nil, losing that one wiring line silently resurrects the R70 defect while the whole suite stays green. Fix autonomously: add an up()-driven lifecycle test with a fake resolver that fails the boot lookup then succeeds — the resolution loop's first step (nextPollAt armed at construction, stepped at DefaultProbeInterval = 200ms) boot-adopts without liveness gating, so asserting IpcGet gains endpoint=<resolved> within ~1-2s needs no probe responder (the egress half stays covered by the existing hand-wired test). ALTERNATIVELY/ADDITIONALLY: make install a REQUIRED newHubFailoverFromSpecs collaborator so a missing production wiring fails fast instead of silently degrading to SetPeerRemote.

RECOMMENDATION: do BOTH if cheap — (a) add the up()-driven production-wiring test that fails fast if ctrl.install is lost, AND (b) make the install collaborator required (remove the silent SetPeerRemote fallback for the boot-adoption path, or assert it non-nil at construction) so the fallback can never silently mask a lost wiring line. If making it strictly required breaks the pure-unit failover/resolution tests that inject their own install, keep those working by having them set the collaborator explicitly (they already do). Mutation-verify: deleting the ctrl.install wiring line in startFailoverAndResolution must fail your new test.

Apply the fix, re-run the gate, commit, return the structured result json with the exact resultCommit sha.

- baboon: Baboon data model compiler reference. - constructive-test-taxonomy: Multi-axis classification of tests (Intention × Encapsulation × Isolation) for evaluating test quality and steering test-writing decisions. Replaces fuzzy Unit/Integration/E2E vocabulary with precise tags and a maintenance-cost heuristic. TRIGGER when: writing new tests, reviewing a test suite, deciding what to test or at what level, discussing mocks vs. real dependencies, classifying tests, or arguing about test coverage or test-strategy tradeoffs.
- dual-tests: Pattern for testing code that touches external systems: write one abstract test suite against an interface, then run it against both the production adapter (real DB/API/FS) and a hand-written in-memory dummy. Prefers manual dummies over auto-generated mocks. TRIGGER when: designing code that depends on a database, HTTP API, message queue, filesystem, or other external system; choosing between mocks, fakes, or dummies; adding integration points; setting up test infrastructure; or when the `constructive-test-taxonomy` skill calls for moving Communication-tier tests toward Blackbox-Atomic.

content.text: Let me examine the key files: failover.go, resolution.go, and the existing tests.

content.content:
1	package device
2	
3	import (
4		"context"
5		"net/netip"
6		"sync"
7		"time"
8	
9		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/config"
10		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/dnsresolve"
11		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/log"
12		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/telemetry"
13	)
14	
15	// nameTarget is one hostname endpoint spec the re-resolution controller tracks: the index of
16	// its owning failoverSpec (which equals the entry's position in config.Peer.EndpointSpecs, since
17	// the failover specs are built 1:1 in TOML order) together with the host/port to resolve. Only
18	// hostname specs (EndpointSpec.IsName) become nameTargets; literal specs never re-resolve.
19	type nameTarget struct {
20		specIdx int
21		host    string
22		port    uint16
23	}
24	
25	// resolution is the edge-side re-resolution controller (Q31/T73). It re-resolves each opted-in
26	// hostname peer-endpoint spec on a fixed poll cadence AND out-of-band the instant every path to
27	// the ACTIVE concentrator goes DOWN, then hands the fresh record set to hubFailover's update API
28	// so the bond is repointed only on an ACTUAL change (D32 suppression lives inside updateResolution).
29	//
30	// It mirrors the hubFailover shape: a pure constructor over injected collaborators (a
31	// dnsresolve.Resolver, the hubFailover whose update API / active-spec identity / shared liveness
32	// plane it drives, a telemetry.Clock, and the [dns] poll interval + per-lookup timeout), so its
33	// decision logic is unit-testable against a fake resolver, a fake clock, and a hubFailover built
34	// over fake health/remote. Resolution runs entirely OFF the send hot path — its own goroutine
35	// performs the network lookups; results are applied only through updateResolution under the
36	// endpoint-set lock (Q34: the two controllers coordinate purely through that shared lock and the
37	// update API, never a second, competing repoint path).
38	//
39	// RETENTION INVARIANT (D46): a lookup that FAILS (error/timeout/NXDOMAIN) or yields an EMPTY
40	// address set NEVER publishes — the spec keeps its last-good expansion and the controller retries
41	// next tick. A working endpoint set is therefore never torn down by a transient resolver fault,
42	// and hubFailover never sees an empty active spec that its total<2 guard could strand the bond on.
43	type resolution struct {
44		resolver dnsresolve.Resolver
45		// fo is the hub-failover controller this resolver drives: the sole mutation point
46		// (updateResolution), the active-spec identity, and the shared liveness plane all live there.
47		fo *hubFailover
48		// targets is the ordered set of hostname specs to re-resolve; empty for an all-literal peer,
49		// in which case the loop is a no-op.
50		targets []nameTarget
51		// families is the set of IP families the local path sockets can source traffic from (a v4
52		// source_addr path reaches only v4 endpoints, a v6 path only v6). A resolved address of a
53		// family NO local path can source is unreachable and is dropped from a spec's expansion — else
54		// an AAAA-only answer on v4-only paths would repoint the bond at an address no path can carry.
55		families pathFamilies
56	
57		clock        telemetry.Clock
58		pollInterval time.Duration
59		timeout      time.Duration
60		log          log.Logger
61	
62		mu sync.Mutex
63		// nextPollAt is when the next SCHEDULED full poll of every hostname spec is due (clock time).
64		// Initialized to construction time so the first step polls immediately (prompt boot adoption
65		// for a hostname-only peer). Advanced by the TTL-clamped poll delay after each poll, and reset
66		// after an out-of-band liveness-loss re-resolve so the two do not double-resolve back to back.
67		// Guarded by mu.
68		nextPollAt time.Time
69		// lastAllDown is the previous liveness-loss reading, so the out-of-band re-resolve is
70		// EDGE-triggered (fires once when hub loss is first observed, not every tick while it
71		// persists — the poll loop covers a sustained outage without hammering the resolver).
72		// Guarded by mu.
73		lastAllDown bool
74	}
75	
76	// newResolution builds the controller over its injected collaborators. It is a pure constructor
77	// (no goroutine, no engine dependency) so a test drives step/pollAll directly against a fake
78	// resolver and a fake clock. nextPollAt is armed at construction time so the first step polls
79	// immediately.
80	func newResolution(resolver dnsresolve.Resolver, fo *hubFailover, targets []nameTarget, families pathFamilies, clock telemetry.Clock, pollInterval, timeout time.Duration, lg log.Logger) *resolution {
81		return &resolution{
82			resolver:     resolver,
83			fo:           fo,
84			targets:      targets,
85			families:     families,
86			clock:        clock,
87			pollInterval: pollInterval,
88			timeout:      timeout,
89			log:          lg,
90			nextPollAt:   clock.Now(),
91		}
92	}
93	
94	// nameTargetsFromSpecs extracts the hostname specs (EndpointSpec.IsName) from an ordered spec
95	// list as nameTargets, tagging each with its index in that list — which equals its failoverSpec
96	// index, since both are built 1:1 in TOML order. Literal specs contribute nothing.
97	func nameTargetsFromSpecs(specs []config.EndpointSpec) []nameTarget {
98		var ts []nameTarget
99		for i, s := range specs {
100			if s.IsName {
101				ts = append(ts, nameTarget{specIdx: i, host: s.Host, port: s.Port})
102			}
103		}
104		return ts
105	}
106	
107	// pathFamilies records which IP families the local path sockets can source traffic from. A path
108	// binds a socket whose family is fixed by its source_addr (bind.listenOnDevice): a v4 source can
109	// only reach v4 endpoints, a v6 source only v6. Re-resolution filters a resolved record set to the
110	// families at least one local path can source, so a lookup answer of an unreachable family (an
111	// AAAA-only answer on a v4-only edge) never repoints the bond at an address no path can carry.
112	type pathFamilies struct {
113		v4 bool
114		v6 bool
115	}
116	
117	// allows reports whether a resolved address's family can be sourced by at least one local path.
118	func (f pathFamilies) allows(a netip.Addr) bool {
119		if a.Unmap().Is4() {
120			return f.v4
121		}
122		return f.v6
123	}
124	
125	// pathFamiliesFromPaths derives the usable-family set from the configured paths. Config validation
126	// guarantees every path carries a valid source_addr (Config.normalize), so each path contributes
127	// exactly one family and the result is non-empty for any peer that reaches this controller.
128	func pathFamiliesFromPaths(paths []config.Path) pathFamilies {
129		var f pathFamilies
130		for _, p := range paths {
131			if p.SourceAddr.Unmap().Is4() {
132				f.v4 = true
133			} else {
134				f.v6 = true
135			}
136		}
137		return f
138	}
139	
140	// step is one evaluation at the probe cadence: it first services a liveness-loss edge (an
141	// out-of-band re-resolve of the active spec the instant every path goes DOWN), then, if the
142	// scheduled poll is due, re-resolves every hostname spec and re-arms the next poll at the
143	// TTL-clamped cadence. It is cheap in the steady state (a liveness read plus a clock compare)
144	// and performs its network lookups off any lock, so a periodic loop may call it at probe cadence.
145	func (r *resolution) step() {
146		r.checkLivenessLoss()
147	
148		r.mu.Lock()
149		due := !r.clock.Now().Before(r.nextPollAt)
150		r.mu.Unlock()
151		if !due {
152			return
153		}
154		delay := r.pollAll()
155		r.mu.Lock()
156		r.nextPollAt = r.clock.Now().Add(delay)
157		r.mu.Unlock()
158	}
159	
160	// pollAll re-resolves every tracked hostname spec once and returns the delay until the next
161	// scheduled poll: the configured poll interval, CLAMPED to the smallest positive TTL reported by
162	// any spec whose transport exposes one (DoH/DoT ttlOk) — the Q31 TTL nice-to-have — so a record
163	// about to expire is re-checked no later than its TTL. A lookup failure or empty result leaves
164	// that spec's expansion untouched (RETENTION INVARIANT) and does not shorten the delay.
165	func (r *resolution) pollAll() time.Duration {
166		delay := r.pollInterval
167		for _, t := range r.targets {
168			if d := r.clampPollDelay(r.resolveTarget(t)); d < delay {
169				delay = d
170			}
171		}
172		return delay
173	}
174	
175	// clampPollDelay returns the scheduled poll interval clamped DOWN to a positive, meaningful TTL,
176	// so a record about to expire is re-checked no later than its TTL (Q31 TTL clamp). A lookup that
177	// exposes no TTL (ttlOk=false — system resolver) or a non-positive one leaves the full interval.
178	// It is applied uniformly to the scheduled poll re-arm AND the out-of-band liveness-loss re-arm,
179	// so the clamp invariant holds on BOTH paths — including the hub-loss window where record
180	// freshness matters most.
181	func (r *resolution) clampPollDelay(minTTL time.Duration, ttlOk bool) time.Duration {
182		if ttlOk && minTTL > 0 && minTTL < r.pollInterval {
183			return minTTL
184		}
185		return r.pollInterval
186	}
187	
188	// resolveTarget performs one context-bounded lookup for a single hostname spec and, on a
189	// non-empty SUCCESS, hands the family-filtered, ordered record set to hubFailover.updateResolution (which
190	// suppresses a no-op change and repoints only on an actual active-IP change). It returns the
191	// lookup's minimum TTL and whether that TTL is meaningful, for the caller's clamp.
192	//
193	// RETENTION INVARIANT (D46): on ANY failure (error/timeout/NXDOMAIN) OR an empty ordered set
194	// (NODATA / a CNAME-only chain / a family the local paths cannot use), it publishes NOTHING —
195	// the spec keeps its previous expansion — and reports (0, false). A previously-resolved hostname
196	// is therefore never collapsed to empty, which is exactly the condition that could strand the
197	// bond under hubFailover's total<2 guard.
198	func (r *resolution) resolveTarget(t nameTarget) (time.Duration, bool) {
199		ctx, cancel := r.lookupContext()
200		defer cancel()
201	
202		addrs, minTTL, ttlOk, err := r.resolver.Lookup(ctx, t.host)
203		if err != nil {
204			r.log.Debug("dns re-resolution: lookup failed; retaining last-good records",
205				"host", t.host, "spec_index", t.specIdx, "error", err.Error())
206			return 0, false
207		}
208		eps := orderAddrPorts(addrs, t.port, r.families)
209		if len(eps) == 0 {
210			r.log.Debug("dns re-resolution: no usable record for local path families; retaining last-good records",
211				"host", t.host, "spec_index", t.specIdx)
212			return 0, false
213		}
214		r.fo.updateResolution(t.specIdx, eps)
215		return minTTL, ttlOk
216	}
217	
218	// checkLivenessLoss fires the out-of-band re-resolve of the ACTIVE spec on the DOWN edge — the
219	// tick at which every path to the active concentrator first reads DOWN. It coordinates with
220	// hubFailover purely through the shared liveness read (allPathsDown) and active-spec identity
221	// (activeSpecIndex): a hub-loss advance and a liveness-loss re-resolve therefore derive from one
222	// detector. The trigger is edge-triggered so a sustained outage re-resolves once here (the poll
223	// loop covers the rest) rather than on every tick.
224	func (r *resolution) checkLivenessLoss() {
225		down := r.fo.allPathsDown()
226	
227		r.mu.Lock()
228		edge := down && !r.lastAllDown
229		r.lastAllDown = down
230		r.mu.Unlock()
231		if !edge {
232			return
233		}
234	
235		active := r.fo.activeSpecIndex()
236		t, ok := r.targetFor(active)
237		if !ok {
238			// The active spec is a literal (or there is no active entry yet): nothing to re-resolve.
239			return
240		}
241		r.log.Warn("dns re-resolution: all paths to active concentrator down; out-of-band re-resolve",
242			"host", t.host, "spec_index", t.specIdx)
243		minTTL, ttlOk := r.resolveTarget(t)
244	
245		// An out-of-band re-resolve subsumes the scheduled poll for this instant; push the next poll
246		// out — but re-arm at the TTL-CLAMPED delay, not a blind full interval, so a short-TTL record
247		// is re-checked no later than its TTL on exactly the hub-loss path where freshness matters most.
248		r.mu.Lock()
249		r.nextPollAt = r.clock.Now().Add(r.clampPollDelay(minTTL, ttlOk))
250		r.mu.Unlock()
251	}
252	
253	// targetFor returns the nameTarget owning specIdx, or ok=false when no tracked hostname spec has
254	// that index (a literal active spec, or specIdx == -1 for no active entry).
255	func (r *resolution) targetFor(specIdx int) (nameTarget, bool) {
256		if specIdx < 0 {
257			return nameTarget{}, false
258		}
259		for _, t := range r.targets {
260			if t.specIdx == specIdx {
261				return t, true
262			}
263		}
264		return nameTarget{}, false
265	}
266	
267	// lookupContext returns a context bounded by the configured per-lookup timeout (a non-positive
268	// timeout yields an unbounded context — the caller's config validation keeps it positive).
269	func (r *resolution) lookupContext() (context.Context, context.CancelFunc) {
270		if r.timeout <= 0 {
271			return context.WithCancel(context.Background())
272		}
273		return context.WithTimeout(context.Background(), r.timeout)
274	}
275	
276	// orderAddrPorts turns a resolver's mixed A/AAAA record set into the ordered, deduplicated
277	// []netip.AddrPort a failoverSpec expansion requires: IPv4 records first (in resolver order),
278	// then IPv6, each paired with the spec's port. The order is DETERMINISTIC so an unchanged DNS
279	// answer produces a byte-identical expansion tick after tick — the precondition for
280	// updateResolution's active-AddrPort survival check to suppress a no-op repoint. Invalid or
281	// duplicate addrs, and addrs of a family NO local path can source (fams), are dropped — so a
282	// record set that filters down to nothing yields an empty expansion the caller retains last-good on.
283	func orderAddrPorts(addrs []netip.Addr, port uint16, fams pathFamilies) []netip.AddrPort {
284		seen := make(map[netip.Addr]struct{}, len(addrs))
285		var v4, v6 []netip.AddrPort
286		for _, a := range addrs {
287			if !a.IsValid() || !fams.allows(a) {
288				continue
289			}
290			canon := a.Unmap()
291			if _, dup := seen[canon]; dup {
292				continue
293			}
294			seen[canon] = struct{}{}
295			ap := netip.AddrPortFrom(canon, port)
296			if canon.Is4() {
297				v4 = append(v4, ap)
298			} else {
299				v6 = append(v6, ap)
300			}
301		}
302		return append(v4, v6...)
303	}
304	
305	// startResolutionLoop launches the re-resolution goroutine: it calls step every interval until
306	// the returned stopper is invoked (idempotent). It mirrors startHubFailoverLoop — a wall-clock
307	// ticker at the probe cadence driving a decision method whose every timing choice runs through
308	// the injected Clock, so a test drives step directly against a fake clock and never starts this
309	// goroutine. It is a no-op (no-op stopper) for a controller with no hostname specs to track or a
310	// non-positive interval.
311	func (r *resolution) startResolutionLoop(interval time.Duration) (stop func()) {
312		if len(r.targets) == 0 || interval <= 0 {
313			return func() {}
314		}
315		done := make(chan struct{})
316		go func() {
317			ticker := time.NewTicker(interval)
318			defer ticker.Stop()
319			for {
320				select {
321				case <-done:
322					return
323				case <-ticker.C:
324					r.step()
325				}
326			}
327		}()
328		var once sync.Once
329		return func() { once.Do(func() { close(done) }) }
330	}

10		awgdevice "github.com/amnezia-vpn/amneziawg-go/device"

18	
19	// hubFailoverSettle is the dwell a newly-selected concentrator endpoint is given to
20	// prove itself LIVE before the controller may advance again. It exists to keep a
21	// STILL-DOWN reading — caused merely by echoes not having returned from the just-
22	// selected hub yet — from skipping straight past a healthy standby.
23	//
24	// It must comfortably exceed the liveness UP-recovery latency: a DOWN path comes UP
25	// after telemetry.DefaultUpSuccesses (3) probe echoes at telemetry.DefaultProbeInterval
26	// (200ms) ≈ 600ms once probes reach a reachable hub. At 3s the dwell clears that with a
27	// wide margin for jitter/RTT, and also bounds the re-advance cadence while a whole hub
28	// fleet is down to at most one switch (one fresh handshake) per 3s. Hub loss is a
29	// coarse, rare event (a concentrator down), distinct from the sub-second PER-PATH
30	// failover the schedulers own, so a switch latency on this scale is acceptable.
31	const hubFailoverSettle = 3 * time.Second
32	
33	// hubHealth is one path's liveness verdict, the read side of the T13 telemetry state
34	// machine (*telemetry.Prober satisfies it). The hub-failover controller reads exactly
35	// the same up/down verdict the schedulers select on, so "hub loss" is derived from the
36	// very liveness plane per-path failover already runs on — no second detector.
37	type hubHealth interface {
38		State() telemetry.PathState
39	}
40	
41	// peerRemote repoints the whole bond's wire remote at a new concentrator endpoint
42	// (*bind.Multipath satisfies it via SetPeerRemote). It is the action half of a hub
43	// switch on the DATA/PROBE plane; the re-handshake is separate (rehandshake).
44	type peerRemote interface {
45		SetPeerRemote(ap netip.AddrPort)
46	}
47	
48	// rehandshake drops the current WireGuard session and initiates a FRESH one against the
49	// peer's static key — no hub-to-hub state handoff. The device wires it to the engine
50	// peer (expire keypairs + send handshake initiation); a test injects a counter.
51	type rehandshake func()
52	
53	// hubFailover is the edge-side active-standby concentrator failover controller (Q18/T57).
54	// It watches the per-path liveness plane and, when EVERY path to the currently-active
55	// concentrator is DOWN simultaneously (HUB LOSS — distinct from a single path failing,
56	// which the schedulers already absorb), advances to the next endpoint in the ordered
57	// concentrator list, repoints every path's remote at it, and initiates a fresh WG
58	// re-handshake. It re-arms against the new endpoint and, if that one is also fully down,
59	// advances again.
60	//
61	// End-of-list policy: WRAP (round-robin modulo the list length). Once the last standby
62	// is exhausted the controller cycles back to index 0 and keeps retrying every endpoint
63	// in order. Wrap is chosen over stop because it preserves availability — a concentrator
64	// that RECOVERS earlier in the list is retried and settled on within one full cycle,
65	// whereas stopping at the last endpoint would strand the edge on a dead hub even after
66	// endpoint 0 came back. The settle dwell bounds the churn to one switch (one handshake)
67	// per hubFailoverSettle while the whole fleet is down, so the round-robin is a slow,
68	// bounded retry, not a storm.
69	//
70	// GUARD (must-hold invariant): a SINGLE-endpoint list takes NO failover action at all —
71	// check returns immediately, so the remote is never repointed and no re-handshake is
72	// initiated. A one-concentrator deployment therefore behaves EXACTLY as it did before
73	// T57 (the endpoints-list "list" is the one-element normalization of the legacy single
74	// endpoint form, per T54).
75	type hubFailover struct {
76		// specs is the ORDERED concentrator endpoint set, one failoverSpec per configured
77		// endpoint entry (config.Peer.EndpointSpecs), in TOML order. Each spec OWNS its
78		// current expansion ([]netip.AddrPort): a literal is a fixed single entry; a hostname
79		// is its latest resolved record set (empty until the resolver — wired by a later task —
80		// first calls updateResolution). The FLATTENED concatenation of every spec's expansion,
81		// in spec order, is the failover order; the flattened index is what `check` advances.
82		// The set is MUTABLE: updateResolution swaps a spec's expansion in place under mu.
83		specs []failoverSpec
84		// health is the per-path liveness, one entry per configured path. Immutable after
85		// construction (the prober set is fixed for the tunnel's life).
86		health []hubHealth
87	
88		remote      peerRemote
89		rehandshake rehandshake
90		// install populates the ENGINE peer's endpoint via the UAPI/IpcSet path (R70). It is used on
91		// the FIRST-RESOLVE INSTALL PATH only — the boot-adoption of a peer that came up endpoint-less —
92		// because SetPeerRemote (remote) repoints the bind's per-path remotes but NEVER sets the engine
93		// peer's endpoint, so after an endpoint-less boot a bare rehandshake would have no endpoint to
94		// transmit to. The device wires it to deviceInstallEndpoint; a unit test over a fake remote with
95		// no engine leaves it nil, and boot adoption then falls back to remote so the switch stays
96		// observable. Subsequent re-resolves of an already-installed peer take the remote (SetPeerRemote)
97		// repoint path — the engine's virtual endpoint stays stable per A1; only the bind remotes move.
98		install func(netip.AddrPort)
99		clock   telemetry.Clock
100		// settle is the dwell a freshly-selected endpoint gets before another advance is
101		// allowed (hubFailoverSettle in production; a test injects its own).
102		settle time.Duration
103		log    log.Logger
104	
105		mu sync.Mutex
106		// activeSpec and activeAddr are the SPEC-SCOPED identity of the ACTIVE endpoint (R70):
107		// the pair (owning spec index, AddrPort) — NEVER a bare flattened index and NEVER a
108		// bare AddrPort. Because a hostname may legitimately re-resolve onto the SAME AddrPort
109		// as another spec's literal (T67's load-time duplicate check is textual host:port
110		// only), duplicate AddrPort VALUES may appear across different specs in the flattened
111		// list; a bare-value re-map could silently bind the active pointer to the wrong spec's
112		// entry, so the active endpoint is addressed by (specIdx, AddrPort). activeSpec == -1
113		// means no active entry yet (every spec's expansion is empty). Guarded by mu.
114		activeSpec int
115		activeAddr netip.AddrPort
116		// idx is the ACTIVE endpoint's position in the FLATTENED list, DERIVED from
117		// (activeSpec, activeAddr) and re-mapped after every mutation. It is a read-only cache
118		// for observation/logging; the source of truth is the spec-scoped identity above. -1
119		// when there is no active entry. Guarded by mu.
120		idx int
121		// lastSwitch is when the active endpoint was last (re)selected — initialized to
122		// construction time so endpoint 0 gets the SAME settle grace at boot that a switched
123		// endpoint gets, preventing a boot-time false failover while probers are still DOWN
124		// before the first echo returns. Guarded by mu.
125		lastSwitch time.Time
126	}
127	
128	// failoverSpec is one ordered endpoint entry together with its CURRENT expansion. A
129	// literal entry's expansion is the fixed single AddrPort; a hostname entry's expansion is
130	// its latest resolved record set (Q32 multi-record), swapped in place by updateResolution.
131	// The spec itself (config.EndpointSpec) is retained so the controller can tell a hostname
132	// spec — which may grow/shrink at runtime — from an immutable literal, e.g. when deciding
133	// whether the failover loop may ever have work to do.
134	type failoverSpec struct {
135		spec  config.EndpointSpec
136		addrs []netip.AddrPort
137	}
138	
139	// newHubFailover builds the controller over an ordered list of IP-LITERAL endpoints — the
140	// legacy all-literal form (each address becomes its own single-entry literal spec). It is
141	// a pure constructor (no goroutine, no engine dependency beyond the injected collaborators)
142	// so its decision logic is unit-testable against a fake clock, fake health, and fake
143	// remote/rehandshake.
144	func newHubFailover(endpoints []netip.AddrPort, health []hubHealth, remote peerRemote, rh rehandshake, clock telemetry.Clock, settle time.Duration, lg log.Logger) *hubFailover {
145		specs := make([]failoverSpec, len(endpoints))
146		for i, ap := range endpoints {
147			specs[i] = failoverSpec{spec: config.EndpointSpec{Addr: ap}, addrs: []netip.AddrPort{ap}}
148		}
149		return newHubFailoverFromSpecs(specs, health, remote, rh, clock, settle, lg)
150	}
151	
152	// newHubFailoverFromSpecs builds the controller over the ordered, spec-keyed endpoint set.
153	// The active endpoint is initialized to the FIRST flattened entry (spec order); when every
154	// spec's expansion is empty (e.g. a hostname-only peer before the resolver has run) there
155	// is no active entry yet (activeSpec == -1) and check takes no action until an expansion
156	// appears. Like newHubFailover it injects no goroutine or engine dependency.
157	func newHubFailoverFromSpecs(specs []failoverSpec, health []hubHealth, remote peerRemote, rh rehandshake, clock telemetry.Clock, settle time.Duration, lg log.Logger) *hubFailover {
158		h := &hubFailover{
159			specs:       specs,
160			health:      health,
161			remote:      remote,
162			rehandshake: rh,
163			clock:       clock,
164			settle:      settle,
165			lastSwitch:  clock.Now(),
166			log:         lg,
167		}
168		h.activeSpec, h.activeAddr = h.entryAtLocked(0)
169		h.idx = h.flatIndexLocked(h.activeSpec, h.activeAddr)
170		return h
171	}
172	
173	// flatLenLocked is the number of endpoints in the FLATTENED list (the sum of every spec's
174	// current expansion length). Caller holds mu.
175	func (h *hubFailover) flatLenLocked() int {
176		n := 0
177		for i := range h.specs {
178			n += len(h.specs[i].addrs)
179		}
180		return n
181	}
182	
183	// canFailoverLocked reports whether the controller could EVER take a failover action: the
184	// flattened list already holds >= 2 endpoints, or SOME spec is a hostname whose runtime
185	// resolution may yet grow it. An all-literal set with a single entry can never grow, so its
186	// loop is a pure no-op and need not run. Caller holds mu.
187	func (h *hubFailover) canFailoverLocked() bool {
188		if h.flatLenLocked() >= 2 {
189			return true
190		}
191		for i := range h.specs {
192			if h.specs[i].spec.IsName {
193				return true
194			}
195		}
196		return false
197	}
198	
199	// flatIndexLocked returns the position of (specIdx, addr) in the FLATTENED list, or -1 if
200	// no such entry exists. It matches BOTH the owning spec index AND the AddrPort — never the
201	// bare value — so a duplicate AddrPort contributed by a DIFFERENT spec is skipped (R70);
202	// within the owning spec the FIRST occurrence wins. Caller holds mu.
203	func (h *hubFailover) flatIndexLocked(specIdx int, addr netip.AddrPort) int {
204		flat := 0
205		for si := range h.specs {
206			for _, a := range h.specs[si].addrs {
207				if si == specIdx && a == addr {
208					return flat
209				}
210				flat++
211			}
212		}
213		return -1
214	}
215	
216	// entryAtLocked maps a FLATTENED index back to its (owning spec index, AddrPort), or
217	// (-1, zero) when flatIdx is out of range (including an empty flattened list). Caller holds mu.
218	func (h *hubFailover) entryAtLocked(flatIdx int) (int, netip.AddrPort) {
219		if flatIdx < 0 {
220			return -1, netip.AddrPort{}
221		}
222		n := 0
223		for si := range h.specs {
224			for _, a := range h.specs[si].addrs {
225				if n == flatIdx {
226					return si, a
227				}
228				n++
229			}
230		}
231		return -1, netip.AddrPort{}
232	}
233	
234	// containsAddrPort reports whether addrs holds target (exact AddrPort equality).
235	func containsAddrPort(addrs []netip.AddrPort, target netip.AddrPort) bool {
236		for _, a := range addrs {
237			if a == target {
238				return true
239			}
240		}
241		return false
242	}
243	
244	// updateResolution swaps specIdx's expansion under h.mu — the sole mutation point of the
245	// endpoint set — and reconciles the active pointer WITHIN its owning spec (R70 spec-scoped
246	// identity):
247	//   - BOOT ADOPTION (activeSpec == -1: every spec's expansion was empty, e.g. a hostname-
248	//     only peer before the resolver first ran): the first resolution that makes the
249	//     flattened list non-empty ADOPTS its head as the active endpoint — set (activeSpec,
250	//     activeAddr), point the bond via exactly ONE SetPeerRemote, one rehandshake, and ARM
251	//     the settle dwell (lastSwitch = now). Without this a single-hostname peer's bond would
252	//     never receive any endpoint: check cannot rescue it either, because a one-record
253	//     expansion keeps the flattened length at 1, permanently under check's total<2 guard.
254	//   - A STANDBY-only change (specIdx != activeSpec) NEVER touches the bond: the active
255	//     entry is untouched; only the DERIVED flattened idx is re-mapped (an earlier spec
256	//     growing/shrinking shifts the active entry's flattened position without moving it).
257	//   - When the ACTIVE spec's own expansion changes: if the active AddrPort SURVIVES the
258	//     swap within that spec, the active entry is stable — strictly NO repoint (Q31/D32
259	//     no-op suppression), only the idx re-maps. If the active AddrPort is GONE, the active
260	//     endpoint's IP has genuinely changed: repoint via exactly ONE SetPeerRemote (D32
261	//     disruptive → Rebaseline + rehandshake) and one rehandshake to the new expansion's
262	//     first entry, and RE-ARM the settle dwell (lastSwitch = now) so the freshly-repointed
263	//     endpoint gets its full grace before a subsequent all-down check may advance off it.
264	//
265	// A change that empties the active spec (nothing to point at) leaves the active identity
266	// stale — the flattened list no longer contains it — and the next check on hub loss
267	// advances off it; no repoint is issued to a non-existent endpoint.
268	func (h *hubFailover) updateResolution(specIdx int, addrs []netip.AddrPort) {
269		h.mu.Lock()
270		defer h.mu.Unlock()
271	
272		if specIdx < 0 || specIdx >= len(h.specs) {
273			return
274		}
275		h.specs[specIdx].addrs = addrs
276	
277		switch {
278		case h.activeSpec == -1:
279			// No active entry yet (every spec was empty at boot). If this resolution populated
280			// the flattened list, adopt its head, point the bond at it, and arm the settle dwell.
281			if adoptSpec, adoptAddr := h.entryAtLocked(0); adoptSpec != -1 {
282				h.activeSpec = adoptSpec
283				h.activeAddr = adoptAddr
284				h.lastSwitch = h.clock.Now()
285				// FIRST-RESOLVE INSTALL PATH (R70): the peer booted endpoint-less, so the engine peer
286				// has NO endpoint yet. INSTALL the resolved endpoint on the engine peer via the UAPI
287				// path (install) FIRST — a SetPeerRemote repoint would move the bind's per-path remotes
288				// but leave the engine peer endpoint-less, so the following rehandshake would have no
289				// endpoint to transmit to — THEN rehandshake, which now has an addressable endpoint.
290				h.installActive(adoptAddr)
291				if h.rehandshake != nil {
292					h.rehandshake()
293				}
294				h.log.Warn("hub failover: first endpoint resolution; installed active concentrator endpoint on the engine peer and re-handshaked",
295					"spec_index", adoptSpec, "to_endpoint", adoptAddr.String())
296			}
297		case specIdx == h.activeSpec:
298			if !containsAddrPort(addrs, h.activeAddr) && len(addrs) > 0 {
299				// The active endpoint's own spec re-resolved off the current AddrPort: its IP
300				// changed. Repoint the bond once, re-handshake against the new first entry, and
301				// re-arm the settle dwell against the freshly-repointed endpoint.
302				prev := h.activeAddr
303				h.activeAddr = addrs[0]
304				h.lastSwitch = h.clock.Now()
305				h.remote.SetPeerRemote(h.activeAddr)
306				if h.rehandshake != nil {
307					h.rehandshake()
308				}
309				h.log.Warn("hub failover: active concentrator endpoint re-resolved; repointed remote and re-handshaked",
310					"spec_index", specIdx, "from_endpoint", prev.String(), "to_endpoint", h.activeAddr.String())
311			}
312			// Survived (or empty expansion): no repoint. idx re-maps below.
313		}
314	
315		h.idx = h.flatIndexLocked(h.activeSpec, h.activeAddr)
316	}
317	
318	// installActive gives the ENGINE peer an addressable endpoint at boot-adoption time (R70). It
319	// prefers the install collaborator (the UAPI/IpcSet endpoint= path, the ONLY way to populate the
320	// engine peer's endpoint); when unset — a unit test driving a fake remote with no engine behind it
321	// — it falls back to SetPeerRemote so the adoption switch stays observable. Caller holds h.mu.
322	func (h *hubFailover) installActive(ap netip.AddrPort) {
323		if h.install != nil {
324			h.install(ap)
325			return
326		}
327		h.remote.SetPeerRemote(ap)
328	}
329	
330	// allPathsDown reports HUB LOSS — every path to the active concentrator DOWN — under h.mu.
331	// It is the read half of the coordination surface the re-resolution controller (T73) shares
332	// with the failover controller (Q34): the resolver reads the SAME liveness sweep the failover
333	// loop advances on, so a liveness-loss out-of-band re-resolve and a hub-loss advance derive
334	// from one detector, never two that could disagree.
335	func (h *hubFailover) allPathsDown() bool {
336		h.mu.Lock()
337		defer h.mu.Unlock()
338		return h.allDownLocked()
339	}
340	
341	// activeSpecIndex returns the owning spec index of the currently-active endpoint under h.mu,
342	// or -1 when there is no active entry yet (every spec's expansion empty). The re-resolution
343	// controller (T73) reads it to know WHICH spec a liveness-loss trigger must re-resolve out of
344	// band; the identity is spec-scoped (R70), so this is the spec index, never a flattened index.
345	func (h *hubFailover) activeSpecIndex() int {
346		h.mu.Lock()
347		defer h.mu.Unlock()
348		return h.activeSpec
349	}
350	
351	// allDownLocked reports HUB LOSS: every path's liveness to the active concentrator is
352	// DOWN simultaneously. An empty health set is NOT hub loss (there is nothing to declare
353	// dead — a bind without the probe transport never drives this). Caller holds mu.
354	func (h *hubFailover) allDownLocked() bool {
355		if len(h.health) == 0 {
356			return false
357		}
358		for _, hp := range h.health {
359			if hp.State() != telemetry.StateDown {
360				return false
361			}
362		}
363		return true
364	}
365	
366	// check is one failover-evaluation step: on confirmed hub loss (and once the active
367	// endpoint's settle dwell has elapsed) it advances to the next endpoint, repoints the
368	// bond's remote, and initiates a fresh re-handshake. It is idempotent and cheap in the
369	// steady state (any path up, or a single-endpoint list) — a length check plus a liveness
370	// sweep — so a periodic loop may call it at the probe cadence.
371	func (h *hubFailover) check() {
372		h.mu.Lock()
373		defer h.mu.Unlock()
374	
375		// GUARD: a FLATTENED list of fewer than two endpoints takes NO failover action — no
376		// advance, no remote repoint, no re-handshake — so a one-concentrator deployment is
377		// byte-for-byte the pre-T57 behaviour, and a hostname peer whose resolution has not yet
378		// yielded a second endpoint waits rather than churning.
379		total := h.flatLenLocked()
380		if total < 2 {
381			return
382		}
383		if !h.allDownLocked() {
384			return
385		}
386		// Settle dwell: give the currently-active endpoint time for its liveness to recover
387		// before advancing, so a not-yet-echoed (transiently DOWN) healthy endpoint is not
388		// skipped. Applies at boot (endpoint 0) and after every switch.
389		now := h.clock.Now()
390		if now.Sub(h.lastSwitch) < h.settle {
391			return
392		}
393	
394		// Advance in FLATTENED order from the active entry's current flattened position. The
395		// position is re-derived from the spec-scoped identity every step, so a standby spec
396		// that grew/shrank since the last advance is walked at its new offset. A missing active
397		// (activeSpec == -1, or its AddrPort no longer present) resumes from the head.
398		prev := h.flatIndexLocked(h.activeSpec, h.activeAddr)
399		var nextIdx int
400		if prev < 0 {
401			nextIdx = 0
402		} else {
403			nextIdx = (prev + 1) % total
404		}
405		nextSpec, next := h.entryAtLocked(nextIdx)
406		h.activeSpec = nextSpec
407		h.activeAddr = next
408		h.idx = nextIdx
409		h.lastSwitch = now
410	
411		// Repoint the DATA/PROBE plane FIRST so probes immediately start re-arming detection
412		// against the new endpoint, then initiate the fresh WG re-handshake toward it.
413		h.remote.SetPeerRemote(next)
414		if h.rehandshake != nil {
415			h.rehandshake()
416		}
417		h.log.Warn("hub failover: all paths to active concentrator down; switched endpoint and re-handshaked",
418			"from_index", prev, "to_index", nextIdx, "to_spec", nextSpec, "to_endpoint", next.String(), "endpoints", total)
419	}
420	
421	// deviceRehandshake returns a rehandshake bound to the engine peer identified by pk: it
422	// EXPIRES the peer's current keypairs (dropping the old hub's session — a fresh session
423	// with NO hub-to-hub state handoff) and sends a fresh handshake initiation, which the
424	// Bind fans out to the just-repointed standby endpoint. All hubs in the ordered set
425	// share the peer's single WireGuard static key, so the SAME peer identity re-handshakes
426	// against whichever hub is now active. A peer the engine cannot resolve (never configured
427	// — impossible for the edge's sole peer) yields a no-op so the loop never dereferences
428	// nil. This is the ONLY engine-peer coupling the failover path takes; it stays in the
429	// device package alongside the rest of the engine wiring.
430	func deviceRehandshake(dev *awgdevice.Device, pk config.Key) rehandshake {
431		key := awgdevice.NoisePublicKey(pk.Bytes())
432		return func() {
433			peer := dev.LookupPeer(key)
434			if peer == nil {
435				return
436			}
437			// Drop the current session so the re-handshake starts a FRESH keypair (no hub-to-hub
438			// handoff); ExpireCurrentKeypairs also backdates lastSentHandshake so the immediately
439			// following initiation is not suppressed by the RekeyTimeout guard.
440			peer.ExpireCurrentKeypairs()
441			_ = peer.SendHandshakeInitiation(false)
442		}
443	}
444	
445	// deviceInstallEndpoint returns an install function that populates the ENGINE peer's endpoint via
446	// the UAPI/IpcSet path — an `endpoint=` line for pk routed through the engine to
447	// Multipath.ParseEndpoint (R70). This is the ONLY way to give the engine peer an addressable
448	// endpoint: SetPeerRemote repoints the bind's per-path remotes but leaves the engine peer's
449	// endpoint untouched, so after an endpoint-less tolerant boot a bare rehandshake has no endpoint
450	// to transmit to. The device installs the FIRST resolved endpoint through this before the first
451	// rehandshake; ParseEndpoint also seeds the (previously remoteless) per-path remotes with it, so
452	// the initiation egresses toward the resolved address. Re-issuing `public_key=` for an existing
453	// peer only updates its endpoint — it does not reset keys or allowed-ips. An IpcSet failure is
454	// logged; the loop retries on the next resolve. It stays in the device package alongside the rest
455	// of the engine wiring, mirroring deviceRehandshake.
456	func deviceInstallEndpoint(dev *awgdevice.Device, pk config.Key, lg log.Logger) func(netip.AddrPort) {
457		raw := pk.Bytes()
458		pubHex := hex.EncodeToString(raw[:])
459		return func(ap netip.AddrPort) {
460			if err := dev.IpcSet(fmt.Sprintf("public_key=%s\nendpoint=%s\n", pubHex, ap.String())); err != nil {
461				lg.Warn("hub failover: failed to install resolved endpoint on the engine peer",
462					"endpoint", ap.String(), "error", err.Error())
463			}
464		}
465	}
466	
467	// peerNeedsHubFailover reports whether a peer's endpoint set warrants a hub-failover
468	// controller: it does when the peer carries ANY hostname spec (its runtime resolution may
469	// yield further endpoints, and even a single hostname can re-resolve onto a new IP the bond
470	// must be repointed at) OR when >= 2 IP-literal endpoints are configured (an ordered
471	// active-standby list). A single-IP-literal peer needs NO controller — it is byte-for-byte
472	// the pre-G5 single-concentrator deployment (Q29), so the failover path never constructs
473	// or runs for it.
474	func peerNeedsHubFailover(peer config.Peer) bool {
475		for _, s := range peer.EndpointSpecs {
476			if s.IsName {
477				return true
478			}
479		}
480		return len(peer.Endpoints) >= 2
481	}
482	
483	// startFailoverAndResolution builds and starts the edge-side hub-failover monitor AND the
484	// re-resolution controller for cfg's concentrator peer, returning their two SEPARATE stoppers
485	// (device.Close invokes both). It returns no-op stoppers when hub failover does not apply: the
486	// concentrator role (it roams edges dynamically and has no endpoint list), a bind without the
487	// probe transport (no probers → no liveness plane), or no peer whose endpoint set warrants a
488	// controller (peerNeedsHubFailover — a single-IP-literal deployment gets none, staying
489	// behaviour-identical to pre-G5). The edge bonds every path to a SINGLE concentrator, so the whole
490	// per-path prober set IS the liveness of the paths to that concentrator (hub loss = every one
491	// DOWN); the controller drives the first qualifying peer.
492	//
493	// The controller's spec expansions come from boot (the bounded initial resolve, Q30): a hostname
494	// that resolved at boot starts with a non-empty expansion (activeSpec set, endpoint already
495	// installed at boot via the UAPI render), while one that did not resolve starts EMPTY (activeSpec
496	// == -1) and the re-resolution loop's first success adopts it through the FIRST-RESOLVE INSTALL
497	// PATH (ctrl.install, R70). The install collaborator is wired here to the engine peer.
498	func startFailoverAndResolution(cfg *config.Config, mp *bind.Multipath, probers []*telemetry.Prober, dev *awgdevice.Device, boot bootEndpoints, lg log.Logger) (stopFailover, stopResolution func()) {
499		noop := func() {}
500		if cfg.Role != config.RoleEdge || len(probers) == 0 {
501			return noop, noop
502		}
503		for i, peer := range cfg.WireGuard.Peers {
504			if !peerNeedsHubFailover(peer) {
505				continue
506			}
507			health := make([]hubHealth, len(probers))
508			for j, pr := range probers {
509				health[j] = pr
510			}
511			hlg := lg.Component("hubfailover")
512			ctrl := newHubFailoverFromSpecs(
513				boot.specs[i], health, mp,
514				deviceRehandshake(dev, peer.PublicKey),
515				telemetry.SystemClock{}, hubFailoverSettle,
516				hlg,
517			)
518			// Wire the engine-endpoint install used by the FIRST-RESOLVE INSTALL PATH (R70): boot
519			// adoption of an endpoint-less peer installs the resolved endpoint on the engine peer here,
520			// then rehandshakes.
521			ctrl.install = deviceInstallEndpoint(dev, peer.PublicKey, hlg)
522			// Poll at the probe cadence: check is cheap (a length check plus a liveness sweep) and the
523			// settle dwell bounds actual switches, so a responsive poll only tightens detection latency
524			// without churning the remote.
525			stopFailover = ctrl.startHubFailoverLoop(telemetry.DefaultProbeInterval)
526			// Alongside failover, start the re-resolution controller (T73) over the SAME ctrl for a
527			// peer carrying hostname specs: it re-resolves each on the [dns] poll cadence and out-of-
528			// band on hub loss, feeding fresh records through ctrl.updateResolution (Q34 — the two
529			// coordinate purely through the shared lock and update API). An all-literal peer has no
530			// hostname to track, so it starts nothing.
531			stopResolution = startResolution(cfg, ctrl, peer, boot.resolver, lg)
532			return stopFailover, stopResolution
533		}
534		return noop, noop
535	}
536	
537	// startResolution builds and starts the re-resolution controller for peer's hostname endpoint
538	// specs over the already-constructed hub-failover controller, or returns a no-op stopper when the
539	// peer carries no hostname spec (an all-literal peer never re-resolves) or the resolver could not
540	// be constructed (nil — logged at boot). The resolver is the one built ONCE at boot from the
541	// (validated) [dns] block and shared with the bounded initial resolve, so hostname re-resolution
542	// never fails tunnel bring-up: hub failover itself is independent of re-resolution.
543	func startResolution(cfg *config.Config, ctrl *hubFailover, peer config.Peer, resolver dnsresolve.Resolver, lg log.Logger) func() {
544		targets := nameTargetsFromSpecs(peer.EndpointSpecs)
545		if len(targets) == 0 || resolver == nil {
546			return func() {}
547		}
548		res := newResolution(
549			resolver, ctrl, targets, pathFamiliesFromPaths(cfg.Paths),
550			telemetry.SystemClock{}, cfg.DNS.PollInterval, cfg.DNS.Timeout,
551			lg.Component("dnsresolve"),
552		)
553		// Drive step at the probe cadence: step is cheap in the steady state (a liveness read plus a
554		// clock compare) and gates the actual poll on the [dns] poll interval internally, so a
555		// responsive tick only tightens the liveness-loss re-resolve latency without over-resolving.
556		return res.startResolutionLoop(telemetry.DefaultProbeInterval)
557	}
558	
559	// startHubFailoverLoop launches the failover-evaluation goroutine: it calls check every
560	// interval until the returned stopper is invoked (idempotent). It mirrors the bind's
561	// StartProbeLoop/StartReconcileLoop lifecycle glue — a wall-clock ticker driving a
562	// decision function whose every timing choice runs through the injected Clock, so a test
563	// drives check directly against a fake clock and never starts this goroutine. It is a
564	// no-op (no-op stopper) for a controller that can NEVER fail over (canFailoverLocked: an
565	// all-literal set with fewer than two entries — no hostname to grow it) or a non-positive
566	// interval. A hostname peer whose expansion is still empty DOES start the loop: its
567	// resolution may later populate a second endpoint, and check no-ops cheaply until then.
568	func (h *hubFailover) startHubFailoverLoop(interval time.Duration) (stop func()) {
569		h.mu.Lock()
570		can := h.canFailoverLocked()
571		h.mu.Unlock()
572		if !can || interval <= 0 {
573			return func() {}
574		}
575		done := make(chan struct{})
576		go func() {
577			ticker := time.NewTicker(interval)
578			defer ticker.Stop()
579			for {
580				select {
581				case <-done:
582					return
583				case <-ticker.C:
584					h.check()
585				}
586			}
587		}()
588		var once sync.Once
589		return func() { once.Do(func() { close(done) }) }
590	}

29	// genX25519 returns a fresh X25519 private/public key pair as raw 32-byte slices — valid curve
30	// material so the engine's CreateMessageInitiation (a DH against the peer static key) succeeds.
31	func genX25519(t *testing.T) (privRaw, pubRaw []byte) {
32		t.Helper()
33		k, err := ecdh.X25519().GenerateKey(rand.Reader)
34		if err != nil {
35			t.Fatalf("generate X25519 key: %v", err)
36		}
37		return k.Bytes(), k.PublicKey().Bytes()
38	}
39	
40	// keyFromRaw builds a config.Key from raw key bytes (via the only public constructor,
41	// UnmarshalText over base64), so a test can pass the SAME key to the engine (as hex UAPI) and to
42	// deviceRehandshake/deviceInstallEndpoint (as a config.Key).
43	func keyFromRaw(t *testing.T, raw []byte) config.Key {
44		t.Helper()
45		var k config.Key
46		if err := k.UnmarshalText([]byte(base64.StdEncoding.EncodeToString(raw))); err != nil {
47			t.Fatalf("build config.Key: %v", err)
48		}
49		return k
50	}
51	
52	// randB64Key returns 32 random bytes base64-encoded (a placeholder PSK / key for a config file).
53	func randB64Key(t *testing.T) string {
54		t.Helper()
55		var b [32]byte
56		if _, err := rand.Read(b[:]); err != nil {
57			t.Fatalf("read random: %v", err)
58		}
59		return base64.StdEncoding.EncodeToString(b[:])
60	}

62	// writeEdgeConfig writes a minimal single-path edge config to a 0600 temp file and loads it. The
63	// endpoints TOML array literal (e.g. `["hub.example.com:51820"]`) and the dns opt-in are the only
64	// per-test variation; every other field is a fixed loopback default.
65	func writeEdgeConfig(t *testing.T, endpointsTOML string, dns bool) *config.Config {
66		t.Helper()
67		privRaw, _ := genX25519(t)
68		_, pubRaw := genX25519(t)
69		dnsLine := ""
70		if dns {
71			dnsLine = "dns = true\n"
72		}
73		body := fmt.Sprintf(`role = "edge"
74	psk = "%s"
75	
76	[[paths]]
77	name = "a"
78	source_addr = "127.0.0.1"
79	
80	[wireguard]
81	private_key = "%s"
82	
83	[[wireguard.peers]]
84	public_key = "%s"
85	%sendpoints = %s
86	allowed_ips = ["10.0.0.0/24"]
87	`, randB64Key(t),
88			base64.StdEncoding.EncodeToString(privRaw),
89			base64.StdEncoding.EncodeToString(pubRaw),
90			dnsLine, endpointsTOML)

92		path := filepath.Join(t.TempDir(), "edge.toml")

94			t.Fatalf("write config: %v", err)
95		}
96		if err := os.Chmod(path, 0o600); err != nil { // defeat umask widening (config.Load requires 0600 exactly)
97			t.Fatalf("chmod config: %v", err)
98		}
99		cfg, err := config.Load(path)
100		if err != nil {
101			t.Fatalf("load config: %v", err)
102		}
103		return cfg
104	}

106	// neverResolver is a resolver whose every lookup returns an error — a name that never resolves.
107	type neverResolver struct{ calls int }
108	
109	func (r *neverResolver) Lookup(_ context.Context, host string) ([]netip.Addr, time.Duration, bool, error) {
110		r.calls++
111		return nil, 0, false, fmt.Errorf("neverResolver: %q does not resolve", host)
112	}

114	// TestUpTolerantBootEndpointless is acceptance (1): Up on a single-hostname peer whose name NEVER
115	// resolves must SUCCEED (tolerant boot, Q30 defer-and-reconcile — never hard-fail on an
116	// unresolvable name), and the engine peer must carry NO endpoint (UAPI get shows no endpoint=
117	// line) — the endpoint-less boot the re-resolution loop later completes on first success.
118	func TestUpTolerantBootEndpointless(t *testing.T) {
119		cfg := writeEdgeConfig(t, `["hub.example.com:51820"]`, true)
120		chtun := tuntest.NewChannelTUN()
121	
122		rslv := &neverResolver{}
123		factoryCalls := 0
124		factory := func() (dnsresolve.Resolver, error) {

126			return rslv, nil
127		}

129		tun, err := up(cfg, discardLogger(t), chtun.TUN(), "wanbondtest0", factory)

132		}

137		}

140		}

142		uapi, err := tun.dev.IpcGet()
143		if err != nil {
144			t.Fatalf("IpcGet: %v", err)
145		}
146		if strings.Contains(uapi, "endpoint=") {
147			t.Fatalf("tolerant boot on an unresolvable hostname installed an endpoint, want NONE (endpoint-less boot):\n%s", uapi)
148		}

152	// all-literal edge peer) constructs NO resolver and starts NO re-resolution loop — asserted via
153	// the wiring seam (the resolver factory is never invoked). The literal endpoint is still installed
154	// on the engine peer at boot, so a single-IP-literal deployment is byte-for-byte the pre-G5 render.
155	func TestUpZeroHostnameNoResolverNoLoop(t *testing.T) {
156		cfg := writeEdgeConfig(t, `["127.0.0.1:51821"]`, false)
157		chtun := tuntest.NewChannelTUN()

159		factoryCalls := 0
160		factory := func() (dnsresolve.Resolver, error) {

162			return &dnsresolve.FakeResolver{Hosts: map[string][]netip.Addr{}}, nil
163		}

165		tun, err := up(cfg, discardLogger(t), chtun.TUN(), "wanbondtest0", factory)

172			t.Fatalf("resolver factory invoked %d times for a zero-hostname config, want 0 (provably inert, Q29)", factoryCalls)
173		}

179		if !strings.Contains(uapi, "endpoint=127.0.0.1:51821") {

184	// TestCloseStopsResolutionLoopNoLeak is acceptance (3): Close stops the re-resolution loop (and the
185	// probe/reconcile/hub-failover loops) with no goroutine leak. goleak verifies zero surviving
186	// goroutines AFTER Close, under -race.
187	func TestCloseStopsResolutionLoopNoLeak(t *testing.T) {
188		defer goleak.VerifyNone(t) // deferred first ⇒ runs LAST, after tun.Close below

190		cfg := writeEdgeConfig(t, `["hub.example.com:51820"]`, true)

193		factory := func() (dnsresolve.Resolver, error) {

202		time.Sleep(3 * telemetry.DefaultProbeInterval)

205		tun.Close()

208	// TestFirstResolveInstallsEndpointAndInitiatesHandshake is acceptance (2), the R70 FIRST-RESOLVE
209	// INSTALL PATH end to end against a REAL engine + real multipath bind (channel TUN, loopback
210	// socket). A hostname-only peer boots endpoint-less; the first successful resolve must (a) INSTALL
211	// the resolved endpoint on the engine peer via the UAPI/IpcSet path — so the engine peer reports it
212	// through UAPI get — AND (b) rehandshake, so a real handshake initiation actually egresses toward
213	// the resolved address (observed at a loopback listener). A fake-rehandshake-counter increment
214	// alone is NOT sufficient (R70): SetPeerRemote never populates the engine peer endpoint, so without
215	// the install the initiation would have no endpoint to transmit to.
216	func TestFirstResolveInstallsEndpointAndInitiatesHandshake(t *testing.T) {
217		lg := discardLogger(t)
218	
219		// A loopback UDP listener stands in for the resolved concentrator address.
220		conc, err := net.ListenUDP("udp", &net.UDPAddr{IP: net.IPv4(127, 0, 0, 1)})

223		}

227		// Real multipath bind over one loopback path with an AlwaysUp scheduler, so the scheduler never
228		// gates the initiation (a real prober would boot DOWN with no responder and Send would drop it).
229		psk := keyFromRaw(t, mustRandom(t, 32))
230		paths := []config.Path{{Name: "a", SourceAddr: netip.MustParseAddr("127.0.0.1")}}
231		scheduler, err := sched.NewActiveBackup([]sched.PathHealth{sched.AlwaysUp{}}, sched.Config{FailbackAfter: time.Second}, telemetry.SystemClock{}, lg)
232		if err != nil {
233			t.Fatalf("build scheduler: %v", err)
234		}
235		mp, err := bind.NewMultipath(paths, psk, scheduler, nil, nil, nil, nil, config.Amnezia{})
236		if err != nil {
237			t.Fatalf("build multipath bind: %v", err)
238		}
239	
240		// Real engine over a channel TUN, brought up endpoint-less (no endpoint= line).
241		chtun := tuntest.NewChannelTUN()
242		dev := awgdevice.NewDevice(chtun.TUN(), mp, engineLogger(lg, "error"))
243		defer dev.Close()
244

249		fmt.Fprintf(&uapi, "public_key=%s\n", hex.EncodeToString(hubPubRaw))
250		fmt.Fprintf(&uapi, "allowed_ip=0.0.0.0/0\n")

253		}
254		if err := dev.Up(); err != nil {
255			t.Fatalf("dev.Up: %v", err)
256		}
257	
258		// The controller as the device wires it: install to the engine peer's UAPI endpoint path,
259		// rehandshake to the engine peer, repoint to the bind. A single hostname spec, EMPTY at boot.
260		hubKey := keyFromRaw(t, hubPubRaw)
261		specs := []failoverSpec{nameSpec("hub.example.com", 51820)}

266	
267		// Before any resolution the engine peer has NO endpoint.
268		if before, err := dev.IpcGet(); err != nil {
269			t.Fatalf("IpcGet (pre-resolve): %v", err)
270		} else if strings.Contains(before, "endpoint=") {
271			t.Fatalf("engine peer had an endpoint before the first resolve, want none:\n%s", before)
272		}
273	
274		// FIRST successful resolve: boot adoption must install on the engine peer and rehandshake.
275		ctrl.updateResolution(0, []netip.AddrPort{concAP})
276	
277		// (a) The engine peer now reports the resolved endpoint via UAPI get.
278		after, err := dev.IpcGet()
279		if err != nil {
280			t.Fatalf("IpcGet (post-resolve): %v", err)
281		}
282		if !strings.Contains(after, "endpoint="+concAP.String()) {
283			t.Fatalf("engine peer endpoint not installed on first resolve, want endpoint=%s:\n%s", concAP, after)
284		}
285	
286		// (b) A real handshake initiation actually egressed toward the resolved address.
287		if err := conc.SetReadDeadline(time.Now().Add(2 * time.Second)); err != nil {
288			t.Fatalf("set read deadline: %v", err)
289		}
290		buf := make([]byte, 4096)
291		n, from, err := conc.ReadFromUDPAddrPort(buf)
292		if err != nil {
293			t.Fatalf("no handshake initiation observed at the resolved address %s (R70: install alone is not enough — the rehandshake must egress): %v", concAP, err)
294		}
295		if n == 0 {
296			t.Fatalf("observed a zero-length datagram from %s, want a framed handshake initiation", from)
297		}
298	}

225	func up(cfg *config.Config, clg log.Logger, tunDev tun.Device, name string, newResolver resolverFactory) (*Tunnel, error) {
226		// Claim the single-amnezia-engine-per-process invariant (D2) BEFORE IpcSet
227		// assigns amneziawg-go's process-global message-type state. On any failure
228		// below, ok stays false and the deferred release returns the hold; the
229		// successful path transfers the hold to the returned Tunnel.
230		if err := globalAmneziaGuard.acquire(cfg.Amnezia); err != nil {
231			_ = tunDev.Close()
232			return nil, err
233		}
234		ok := false
235		defer func() {
236			if !ok {
237				globalAmneziaGuard.release(cfg.Amnezia)
238			}
239		}()
240	
241		// One live *telemetry.Prober per path drives on-wire liveness. The SAME prober
242		// values back the scheduler's PathHealth AND the bind's probe transport, so the
243		// liveness the probe loop measures is exactly the liveness the scheduler selects
244		// on (T37 replaces the sched.AlwaysUp placeholder here).
245		scheduler, probers, newProber, err := buildScheduler(cfg, clg)
246		if err != nil {
247			_ = tunDev.Close()
248			return nil, fmt.Errorf("device: build scheduler: %w", err)
249		}
250		mpBind, err := bind.NewMultipath(cfg.Paths, cfg.PSK, scheduler, probers, newProber, fecConfig(cfg.FEC), adaptiveFECConfig(cfg.FEC), cfg.Amnezia)
251		if err != nil {
252			_ = tunDev.Close()
253			return nil, fmt.Errorf("device: build multipath bind: %w", err)
254		}
255		dev := awgdevice.NewDevice(tunDev, mpBind, engineLogger(clg, cfg.Log.Level))
256	
257		// Bounded initial hostname resolution (Q30): construct the resolver ONCE (only when some peer
258		// carries a hostname spec — Q29 inertness), resolve each hostname spec under a short timeout,
259		// and seed its expansion. The per-peer boot endpoint — the flattened head of the seeded specs —
260		// is what the UAPI render installs on the engine peer; a hostname that does not resolve in the
261		// boot window (or a resolver that fails to construct) leaves its spec EMPTY and the peer boots
262		// WITHOUT a peer endpoint (tolerant boot). The re-resolution loop then completes the wiring on
263		// first success via the FIRST-RESOLVE INSTALL PATH (R70). Literal specs are seeded verbatim, so
264		// an all-literal peer boots byte-for-byte as before.
265		boot := resolveBootEndpoints(cfg, newResolver, clg)
266	
267		uapi, err := uapiConfig(cfg, boot.endpoints)
268		if err != nil {

271		}
272		if err := dev.IpcSet(uapi); err != nil {

274			return nil, fmt.Errorf("device: apply UAPI config: %w", err)
275		}
276		if err := dev.Up(); err != nil {
277			dev.Close()
278			return nil, fmt.Errorf("device: bring up: %w", err)
279		}
280	
281		// The engine has opened the bind (dev.Up → BindUpdate → Open), so the per-path
282		// sockets exist: start the probe cadence now. Close stops it before dev.Close.
283		// The interval is the SINGLE-SOURCE-OF-TRUTH telemetry default, which also arms
284		// the bind's receive-path liveness sweep throttle (D15).
285		stopProbes := mpBind.StartProbeLoop(telemetry.DefaultProbeInterval)
286		// Start the background deferred-path reconciler alongside probing (T55): a path the
287		// tolerant Open left DOWN (its source_addr not yet assignable — a 5G modem without a
288		// lease at boot) is retried and promoted to a live path as its address appears,
289		// WITHOUT a tunnel restart. Close stops it before dev.Close, like the probe loop.
290		stopReconcile := mpBind.StartReconcileLoop(bind.DefaultReconcileInterval)
291		// Start the edge-side hub-failover monitor (T57) AND the DNS re-resolution loop (T74): on hub
292		// loss (every path to the active concentrator DOWN) failover advances to the next ordered peer
293		// endpoint, repoints the bond's remote, and re-handshakes; re-resolution re-resolves opted-in
294		// hostname specs and installs/repoints on change. Both a no-op on the concentrator, a
295		// single-IP-literal edge, or a bind without the probe transport. Started AFTER dev.Up so the
296		// engine peer the re-handshake / endpoint-install look up exists (IpcSet added it above). Close
297		// stops both before dev.Close, like the probe/reconcile loops.
298		stopHubFailover, stopResolution := startFailoverAndResolution(cfg, mpBind, probers, dev, boot, clg)
299	
300		t := &Tunnel{
301			dev: dev, tun: tunDev, name: name, bind: mpBind, cfg: cfg, log: clg,
302			stopProbes: stopProbes, stopReconcile: stopReconcile,
303			stopHubFailover: stopHubFailover, stopResolution: stopResolution, amnezia: cfg.Amnezia,

12	// across the codebase inject it in place of SystemResolver.
13	type FakeResolver struct {
14		// Hosts maps a hostname to the addrs Lookup returns for it, in order.
15		Hosts map[string][]netip.Addr
16		// MinTTL and TTLOk are returned verbatim for every mapped host.
17		MinTTL time.Duration
18		TTLOk  bool
19	}

22	
23	// Lookup implements Resolver: it returns the addrs mapped to host, or a
24	// non-nil error when host is not in Hosts.
25	func (f *FakeResolver) Lookup(ctx context.Context, host string) ([]netip.Addr, time.Duration, bool, error) {
26		if err := ctx.Err(); err != nil {
27			return nil, 0, false, err
28		}
29		addrs, ok := f.Hosts[host]
30		if !ok {
31			return nil, 0, false, fmt.Errorf("dnsresolve: no such host %q", host)
32		}
33		return addrs, f.MinTTL, f.TTLOk, nil
34	}
